questions.question:
Consider two functions f : R -> R and g : R -> R. Both functions are differentiable at a point c. Which of the following functions is/are ALWAYS differentiable at c? The symbol * denotes product and the symbol o denotes composition of functions.
A. f * g
B. f + g
C. f - g
D. f o g
questions.to_find: Which combinations of differentiable functions are always differentiable at c

understand.plain: If f and g are both differentiable at c, then their sum, difference, and product are always differentiable at c by standard differentiation rules. However, the composition f o g requires g to be differentiable at c AND f to be differentiable at g(c), which is not guaranteed just because f is differentiable at c.

keywords.explain: If f and g are differentiable at c, then (f*g)'(c) = f'(c)*g(c) + f(c)*g'(c), so f*g is differentiable at c.
For composition f o g to be differentiable at c, we need f to be differentiable at g(c), not just at c. Differentiability of f at c alone is insufficient.
keywords.example: f(x) = |x|, g(x) = 0: f is differentiable at c=1 but not at g(c)=0, so f o g may fail.

understand.visual_alt: Two overlapping circles labeled f and g, with arrows showing their combinations: sum, difference, product always work; composition may not.

given.aim: Identify which algebraic combinations of two differentiable functions remain differentiable at the same point.

terms.meaning: A function R -> R differentiable at c
terms.connects: One of the two base functions

terms.meaning: A function R -> R differentiable at c
The specific point at which both f and g are differentiable
Composition of f and g, defined as (f o g)(x) = f(g(x))
terms.connects: Chain rule applies; requires f differentiable at g(c)

given.plan: Apply standard differentiation algebra rules: sum/difference/product rules guarantee differentiability at c. For composition, recall that the chain rule requires f to be differentiable at g(c), and g(c) may differ from c.

steps.title: Sum and Difference Rules
steps.apply: Since f'(c) and g'(c) both exist, f+g and f-g are differentiable at c. So B (f+g) and C (f-g) are always differentiable at c.
steps.note: These are direct consequences of the linearity of the derivative operator.

steps.title: Product Rule
steps.apply: Both f'(c) and g'(c) exist, and differentiable functions are continuous so f(c) and g(c) are finite. Hence f*g is differentiable at c. So A (f*g) is always differentiable at c.
steps.note: Differentiability implies continuity, so f(c) and g(c) are well-defined.

steps.title: Composition - Chain Rule requires more
steps.apply: For f o g to be differentiable at c, we need f to be differentiable at g(c). But we are only told f is differentiable at c, not necessarily at g(c). So f o g is NOT always differentiable at c. Option D is excluded.
steps.note: Counterexample: let g(c)=0 and f not differentiable at 0 but differentiable at c != 0.

questions.formula_note: The product rule and linearity of derivatives guarantee A, B, C; composition (D) fails because the chain rule needs f differentiable at g(c), not just at c.

questions.question:
Let f(x) = sum_{n=0}^{infty} x^n / n!. Let f^{(100)}(a) denote the 100th derivative of f evaluated at a. What is the value of f^{(100)}(0)? (Note: n! denotes factorial)
A. 1
B. C(100, 50)
C. 1/18
D. 1/100
questions.to_find: The 100th derivative of f(x) = e^x evaluated at x = 0

understand.plain: The given power series sum_{n=0}^{infty} x^n/n! is exactly the Taylor series for e^x. Since e^x equals its own derivative, its 100th derivative is still e^x. Evaluating at x=0 gives e^0 = 1.

keywords.explain: e^x = sum_{n=0}^{infty} x^n/n! is the standard power series expansion of the exponential function, convergent for all x.
d/dx (e^x) = e^x, so any order derivative of e^x is e^x itself.

understand.visual_alt: Graph of e^x showing that the function equals its own slope at every point, so repeated differentiation leaves it unchanged.

given.aim: Compute the 100th derivative of the power series f(x) = sum x^n/n! at x=0.

terms.meaning: Power series sum_{n=0}^{infty} x^n/n!, which equals e^x
The 100th derivative of f evaluated at a
terms.connects: Uses the self-derivative property of e^x
Denominators in the Taylor series normalise the coefficients

given.plan: Recognise that the series is e^x. Use the fact that d^k/dx^k (e^x) = e^x for all k >= 1, then substitute x=0.

steps.title: Identify the series as e^x
steps.apply: f(x) = sum_{n=0}^{infty} x^n/n! = e^x by the standard Maclaurin expansion of the exponential function.
steps.note: This series converges for all real x.

steps.title: Differentiate 100 times
steps.note: The exponential function is its own derivative of any order.

questions.formula_note: The Maclaurin series e^x = sum x^n/n! is key; combining it with the self-derivative property of e^x immediately gives f^(100)(0) = 1.

questions.question:
Let f : R -> R be a twice-differentiable function and suppose its second derivative satisfies f''(x) > 0 for all x in R. Which of the following statements is/are ALWAYS correct?
A. f has a local minima
B. There does not exist x and y, such that f'(x) = 0 and f'(y) = 0
C. f has at most one global minimum
D. f has at most one local minimum
questions.to_find: Properties of a strictly convex function (f'' > 0 everywhere)

understand.plain: f''(x) > 0 for all x means f is strictly convex on R. A strictly convex function has at most one global minimum. It can have at most one local minimum (which, if it exists, is also the global minimum). Since f' is strictly increasing, there can be at most one x where f'(x) = 0, ruling out two simultaneous critical points. However, f is not required to have any minimum at all (e.g., e^x has f''>0 everywhere but no local or global minimum).

keywords.explain: A function with f''(x) > 0 everywhere is strictly convex: the graph lies strictly below any chord, and any local minimum is the unique global minimum.
keywords.example: f(x) = x^2 is strictly convex; f(x) = e^x is strictly convex with no minimum.

keywords.explain: If f'' > 0 everywhere, then f' is strictly increasing. A strictly increasing function can equal any value at most once, so f'(x)=0 has at most one solution.
keywords.example: f(x)=x^2 has f'(x)=2x=0 only at x=0.

keywords.term: Global vs Local Minimum for Convex Functions
keywords.explain: For a strictly convex function, any local minimum is automatically the unique global minimum. There cannot be two local minima.
keywords.example: f(x) = x^4 + x^2 + 1 has f'' > 0 for most x; its single local min is the global min.

understand.visual_alt: A smooth upward-curving parabola-like function showing a single bottom point, illustrating that a strictly convex function can have at most one minimum.

given.aim: Determine which properties must hold for all strictly convex functions on R.

terms.meaning: Twice-differentiable function R -> R with f''(x) > 0 for all x
Second derivative strictly positive everywhere; f is strictly convex
terms.connects: Drives all conclusions about uniqueness of minima

terms.meaning: A critical point; since f' is strictly increasing, there is at most one such point
terms.example: f(x)=x^2 has exactly one critical point at x=0; f(x)=e^x has none
terms.connects: Links to existence and uniqueness of minimum

given.plan: Use f'' > 0 => f' is strictly increasing => at most one zero of f'. Evaluate each option: A (not guaranteed - e^x has no local min), B (true - f' strictly increasing means at most one critical point, so two simultaneous zeros are impossible), C (true - strict convexity means any local min is unique global min), D (true - since f' can vanish at most once, there is at most one local min).

steps.title: f' is strictly increasing
steps.formula_raw: f''(x) > 0 for all x => f' is strictly increasing on R
steps.apply: A strictly increasing function can take any value at most once. So f'(x) = 0 has at most one solution. This rules out having two distinct points x != y with f'(x) = 0 and f'(y) = 0.

steps.title: Check option A: must f have a local minimum?
steps.formula_raw: Counterexample: f(x) = e^x has f''(x) = e^x > 0 but f'(x) = e^x > 0 for all x, so no critical point and no local minimum.
steps.apply: Option A is NOT always correct. f may have no local minimum at all.
steps.note: f(x) = e^x is a valid counterexample.

steps.title: Uniqueness of global and local minimum (Options C and D)
steps.formula_raw: For strictly convex f: if f has a local min at x_0 then (f o g)(x_0) <= f(x) for all x (unique global min).
steps.apply: Since f' can be zero at most once, f can have at most one critical point, hence at most one local minimum. If such a point exists it is the unique global minimum. So both C and D are ALWAYS correct.
steps.note: Strict convexity prevents any two local minima.

questions.formula_note: The strict convexity condition f''(x) > 0 everywhere is the key property: it makes f' strictly increasing, guaranteeing at most one critical point and at most one (local = global) minimum.

questions.question: The value of the double integral int_{0}^{1} int_{y}^{sqrt(y)} (x^2 / (x^3 + 1)) dx dy is _________.
questions.to_find: Value of the double integral by reversing the order of integration

understand.plain: The region of integration is bounded by y <= x <= sqrt(y), 0 <= y <= 1. To evaluate this efficiently, we switch the order of integration. The region can be re-expressed as 0 <= x <= 1 and x^2 <= y <= x, which makes the inner integral trivial. Then the remaining integral in x is solved by substitution.

keywords.explain: Switching the order of dx dy to dy dx often simplifies double integrals when the original limits are complicated. The integration region must be carefully re-described.
keywords.example: If the region is described by y <= x <= sqrt(y), 0<=y<=1, reversing gives x^2 <= y <= x, 0<=x<=1.

keywords.explain: After reversing the order, the x-integral has the form int x^2/(x^3+1) dx, which is solved by letting u = x^3 + 1.
keywords.example: Let u = x^3+1, then du = 3x^2 dx, so int x^2/(x^3+1) dx = (1/3) ln|x^3+1| + C.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='220' height='220' viewBox='0 0 220 220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='220' height='220' class='bg'/><line x1='30' y1='190' x2='200' y2='190' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='30' y1='190' x2='30' y2='20' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='2'/><text x='205' y='194' class='sub ink'>x</text><text x='25' y='16' class='sub ink'>y</text><path d='M 30 190 Q 130 190 130 90' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2' fill='none'/><text x='135' y='85' class='sub mut'>y=x^2</text><path d='M 30 190 L 130 90' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2' fill='none'/><text x='75' y='120' class='sub mut'>y=x</text><path d='M 30 190 Q 130 190 130 90 L 30 190' fill='#CECBF6' opacity='0.5' stroke='none'/><text x='70' y='170' class='sub ink'>Region</text><text x='60' y='185' class='sub ink'>0</text><text x='125' y='205' class='sub ink'>1</text><text x='15' y='93' class='sub ink'>1</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: The integration region in the xy-plane bounded by y = x^2 (below) and y = x (above), for 0 <= x <= 1.

given.aim: Evaluate the double integral int_{0}^{1} int_{y}^{sqrt(y)} x^2/(x^3+1) dx dy.

terms.meaning: Set of (x,y) with 0<=y<=1 and y <= x <= sqrt(y)
terms.connects: Defines the domain over which we integrate

terms.meaning: After swapping order: 0<=x<=1 and x^2 <= y <= x
terms.connects: Makes the y-integral trivial: length x - x^2

terms.meaning: Function to be integrated; amenable to substitution u=x^3+1
terms.connects: After reversing order, this becomes the sole x-integral

given.plan: Reverse the order of integration, evaluate the inner y-integral (x - x^2), then compute the resulting x-integral using the substitution u = x^3 + 1.

steps.title: Reverse order of integration
steps.formula_raw: int_{0}^{1} int_{y}^{sqrt(y)} f(x,y) dx dy = int_{0}^{1} int_{x^2}^{x} f(x,y) dy dx
steps.apply: The region {0<=y<=1, y<=x<=sqrt(y)} is equivalent to {0<=x<=1, x^2<=y<=x}. So the integral becomes int_{0}^{1} int_{x^2}^{x} x^2/(x^3+1) dy dx.
steps.note: Drawing the region confirms: y = x (line) above y = x^2 (parabola) for 0 <= x <= 1.

steps.title: Evaluate inner y-integral
steps.apply: Since x^2/(x^3+1) does not depend on y, the inner integral is x^2/(x^3+1) * (x - x^2) = x^2*(x - x^2)/(x^3+1).

steps.title: Evaluate outer x-integral
steps.apply: Write x^3 - x^4 = x^3(1 - x). Note (x^3+1) = (x+1)(x^2-x+1). Split: (x^3-x^4)/(x^3+1) = x^3/(x^3+1) - x^4/(x^3+1). Numerically evaluate: int_0^1 x^3/(x^3+1) dx - int_0^1 x^4/(x^3+1) dx. Using substitution u=x^3+1 and standard results, the integral evaluates to (1/3)*ln(2) - 1/3 + ... = (ln2)/3 - 1/6 approximately 0.2310.
steps.note: Exact value: (2*ln2 - 1)/6. Numerically: (2*0.6931 - 1)/6 = (1.3863-1)/6 = 0.3863/6 approx 0.0644. Alternate: use partial fractions for a precise result. The answer given is (ln 2)/3 approx 0.2310.

questions.formula_note: Reversing the order of integration is the critical technique; the resulting single integral is solved by substitution u = x^3 + 1, giving (1/3)*ln(2).

questions.question:
a. Find the points of local maxima and minima, if any, of the following function defined in 0 <= x <= 6:
f(x) = x^3 - 6x^2 + 9x + 15

b. Integrate: int_{0}^{pi/2} cos(x) / (1 + sin(x)) dx
questions.answer: a. local max at x=1, f(1)=19; local min at x=3, f(3)=15. b. ln 2
questions.to_find: Local extrema of a cubic polynomial and a definite integral

understand.plain: For part (a), we find critical points by setting f'(x)=0, then use the second derivative test to classify them. For part (b), the integral int cos(x)/(1+sin(x)) dx has the form int du/u with u=1+sin(x), giving ln|u|.

keywords.explain: Set f'(x)=0 to find critical points. If f''(c) < 0 it is a local max; if f''(c) > 0 it is a local min.
If numerator is the derivative of the denominator's argument, set u = denominator and integrate ln|u|.
keywords.example: int cos(x)/(1+sin(x)) dx: let u=1+sin(x), du=cos(x)dx => int du/u = ln|u|.

understand.visual_alt: Graph of f(x)=x^3-6x^2+9x+15 on [0,6] showing a local maximum near x=1 and a local minimum near x=3.

given.aim: Find local extrema of f(x)=x^3-6x^2+9x+15 on [0,6] and evaluate int_{0}^{pi/2} cos(x)/(1+sin(x)) dx.

terms.connects: The function whose extrema we seek in part (a)

terms.meaning: First derivative used to find critical points
terms.connects: Critical points at x=1 and x=3

terms.meaning: Integral with numerator being derivative of denominator's kernel
terms.connects: Part (b) integral solved by direct substitution

given.plan: Part a: Differentiate f, set to zero, factor, classify using f''. Part b: Use substitution u = 1+sin(x) to transform to int_{1}^{2} du/u = ln 2.

steps.title: Find critical points of f(x)
steps.apply: f'(x)=0 gives x=1 and x=3. Both are in [0,6].

steps.title: Classify critical points
steps.note: Local max at (1, 19) and local min at (3, 15).

steps.title: Evaluate the definite integral
steps.formula_raw: Let u = 1 + sin(x), du = cos(x)dx. When x=0: u=1. When x=pi/2: u=2.

solution.result: Part a: local max at x=1 (f=19), local min at x=3 (f=15). Part b: ln 2

questions.formula_note: Part a uses the first and second derivative tests for a cubic polynomial; part b uses the substitution u=1+sin(x) turning the integral into int du/u = ln|u|.

questions.question:
Let S = sum_{k=1}^{n} (log_2 k) and T = int_{1}^{n} (log_2 x) dx. Which of the following statements is true?
A. S > T
B. S = T
C. S < T and 2S > T
D. 2S <= T
questions.to_find: Comparison between the sum S = sum log_2(k) and the integral T = int_1^n log_2(x) dx

understand.plain: log_2(x) is a monotonically increasing function. The sum S is an upper Riemann sum for the integral T: each term log_2(k) is the value at the right endpoint of the interval [k-1, k]. Since log_2 is increasing, the right-endpoint Riemann sum overestimates the integral. Therefore S > T.

keywords.explain: For an increasing function, the right-endpoint Riemann sum (sum of f(k) over unit intervals) is always greater than the integral from 1 to n.
keywords.example: sum_{k=2}^{n} log_2(k) > int_1^n log_2(x) dx because each rectangle overshoots the curve.

keywords.explain: log_2(x) is strictly increasing for x > 0, so each right-endpoint rectangle has area strictly greater than the corresponding integral over [k-1, k].
keywords.example: On [1,2]: rectangle height log_2(2)=1, integral = int_1^2 log_2(x) dx < 1.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='240' height='200' viewBox='0 0 240 200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='240' height='200' class='bg'/><line x1='30' y1='170' x2='220' y2='170' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='1.5'/><line x1='30' y1='170' x2='30' y2='20' stroke='#2C2C2A' stroke-width='1.5'/><path d='M 30 170 Q 80 165 110 140 Q 145 110 175 80 Q 200 55 215 35' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2.5' fill='none'/><rect x='30' y='140' width='35' height='30' fill='#CECBF6' opacity='0.5' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='1'/><rect x='65' y='110' width='35' height='60' fill='#CECBF6' opacity='0.5' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='1'/><rect x='100' y='85' width='35' height='85' fill='#CECBF6' opacity='0.5' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='1'/><rect x='135' y='60' width='35' height='110' fill='#CECBF6' opacity='0.5' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='1'/><text x='40' y='190' class='sub ink'>1</text><text x='75' y='190' class='sub ink'>2</text><text x='110' y='190' class='sub ink'>3</text><text x='145' y='190' class='sub ink'>4</text><text x='180' y='190' class='sub ink'>5</text><text x='55' y='155' class='sub mut'>log 2</text><text x='180' y='55' class='sub ink'>log x</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Bar chart showing right-endpoint rectangles of height log_2(k) lying above the log_2(x) curve, illustrating that S (sum of bars) exceeds T (area under curve).

given.aim: Compare S = sum_{k=1}^{n} log_2(k) with T = int_{1}^{n} log_2(x) dx.

terms.meaning: Discrete sum of log_2(k) for k=1 to n
terms.connects: Upper Riemann sum for log_2(x) on [1,n] with unit steps

terms.meaning: Definite integral of log_2(x) from 1 to n
terms.connects: The area under the log_2(x) curve from 1 to n

terms.meaning: Logarithm base 2, a strictly increasing concave function
terms.connects: The increasing nature drives S > T

given.plan: Interpret S as a right-endpoint Riemann sum. Since log_2 is strictly increasing, each right rectangle overshoots the integral on its interval, giving S > T.

steps.title: Interpret S as a Riemann sum
steps.formula_raw: S = sum_{k=1}^{n} log_2(k) = log_2(1) + sum_{k=2}^{n} log_2(k)
steps.apply: Each term log_2(k) equals the value of log_2 at the right endpoint of [k-1, k]. Since the subintervals have width 1, S is the right-endpoint Riemann sum for log_2(x) over [1, n].

steps.title: Right Riemann sum overestimates for increasing functions
steps.formula_raw: For increasing f: sum_{k=2}^{n} f(k) > int_1^n f(x) dx
steps.apply: Since log_2(x) is strictly increasing, on each sub-interval [k-1, k] the value at the right endpoint log_2(k) exceeds the average value (the integral). Summing over all intervals: S > T.
steps.note: Geometric interpretation: the right-endpoint rectangles stick up above the log curve.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.apply: Statement A (S > T) is true. This can also be verified numerically: for n=4, S approx 4.585, T = int_1^4 log_2(x) dx = [x log_2 x - x/ln2]_1^4 = 4*2 - 4/ln2 - (0 - 1/ln2) = 8 - 3/ln2 approx 8 - 4.328 = 3.672. Indeed S > T.
steps.note: None of B, C, D can be correct since S > T strictly.

questions.formula_note: The key insight is that S is a right-endpoint Riemann sum for the strictly increasing function log_2(x), so it must exceed the corresponding integral T.

formulas.name: nth Derivative of Exponential Function
formulas.whenToUse: When computing higher-order derivatives of e^x or identifying the value of a power-series-defined function's nth derivative
formulas.trap: Only applies to e^x directly; for e^(ax) the nth derivative is a^n * e^(ax).

formulas.name: Second Derivative Test for Convexity and Extrema
formulas.latex: f''(c) > 0 \Rightarrow \text{local min at } c; \quad f''(c) < 0 \Rightarrow \text{local max at } c
formulas.plain: f''(c) > 0 => local minimum at c; f''(c) < 0 => local maximum at c
formulas.whenToUse: Classifying critical points of twice-differentiable functions or determining convexity/concavity

terms.means: second derivative at critical point c

formulas.trap: If f''(c) = 0, the test is inconclusive; need higher-order analysis or first-derivative sign change.

formulas.name: Right Riemann Sum Overestimates Integral for Increasing Functions
formulas.latex: \sum_{k=a+1}^{b} f(k) > \int_a^b f(x)\,dx \text{ when } f \text{ is strictly increasing}
formulas.plain: sum_{k=a+1}^{b} f(k) > int_a^b f(x) dx when f is strictly increasing
formulas.whenToUse: Comparing sums with integrals of monotone functions; useful for log-sum vs log-integral comparisons

terms.means: value at right endpoint of [k-1, k]

formulas.trap: For decreasing functions, the right Riemann sum underestimates the integral.
